params.text:
Why complaints are valuable

It is easy to see a complaint as criticism, but a complaint is really a gift. It tells you where care could be better, gives the organisation a chance to put things right, and helps prevent the same problem happening to someone else. Services that welcome complaints tend to be safer and more responsive than those that discourage them.

Everyone has the right to raise a concern or complaint, and to have it taken seriously without fear of being treated differently as a result.

Responding to comments and complaints

When someone raises a comment or complaint, you should:

  • listen carefully, stay calm and do not become defensive
  • take it seriously, whatever your personal view of the issue
  • apologise for any distress caused, and thank them for raising it
  • tell them what will happen next and who will deal with it
  • pass it on according to your organisation's complaints procedure and record it accurately

You do not need to have all the answers yourself. What matters is that the concern is heard, recorded and passed to the right person so it can be looked into properly.

params.taskDescription: Complete the sentences about handling comments and complaints by dragging each word into the correct gap.
params.textField: A complaint should be seen as a *gift* because it helps a service improve. When someone complains you should *listen* carefully, stay calm and never become *defensive*. You should *apologise* for any distress caused, explain what happens *next*, and *record* the complaint according to your organisation's procedure.
